Dynamic Reference Frame.category_description: Evaluate whether the model can correctly represent the coordination between relative and absolute motion in moving reference frames — for example, observing surrounding objects from a moving vehicle or a rotating platform.

prompts.prompt_en: A person looks out from a moving train at trees passing by in the opposite direction.
prompts.description: Tests whether the model can represent relative motion between the observer and the external world.

evaluation.overall_instruction: Answer 'Yes' or 'No' to each question and briefly explain your reasoning for each.
evaluation.evaluation_description: Three reasoning layers are assessed: (1) Scene & viewpoint consistency (q1–q2). (2) Relative motion coherence (q3–q5). (3) Physical & temporal realism (q6–q8).

evaluation_questions.question: Is the viewpoint clearly identifiable as being inside or associated with a moving system (moving train interior)?
Does the camera perspective remain consistent with a view from within a moving train?
Are relative motion directions between observer and background correct (opposite-direction background motion)?
Is the motion speed and direction physically plausible (opposite-direction background motion)?
Is parallax or motion depth correctly represented (opposite-direction background motion)?
Does the perspective of the passing trees shift appropriately as the train moves, maintaining a coherent view of their relative position over time?
Are motions temporally consistent and smooth without abrupt reversals (smooth, continuous realism)?
Does the background scenery of trees remain proportionally scaled and correctly oriented relative to the viewer's motion throughout the video?

prompts.prompt_en: Children ride on a rotating carousel while the background moves in circles.
prompts.description: Tests whether the model can generate relative motion within a rotating frame.

evaluation.overall_instruction: Answer 'Yes' or 'No' to each question and briefly explain your reasoning for each.
evaluation.evaluation_description: Three reasoning layers are assessed: (1) Scene & viewpoint consistency (q1–q2). (2) Relative motion coherence (q3–q5). (3) Physical & temporal realism (q6–q8).

evaluation_questions.question: Is the viewpoint clearly identifiable as being inside or associated with a moving system (rotating carousel)?
Is the external background visible and contrasting with the reference frame (rotating carousel)?
Are relative motion directions between observer and background correct (circular background rotation)?
Is the motion speed and direction physically plausible (circular background rotation)?
Is parallax or motion depth correctly represented (circular background rotation)?
Does the internal frame remain stable relative to itself (rotational motion realism)?
Are motions temporally consistent and smooth without abrupt reversals (rotational motion realism)?
Do the children’s positions and orientations remain consistent relative to the rotating carousel throughout the video?

prompts.prompt_en: A drone flies forward while filming a car driving in the same direction.
prompts.description: Tests whether the model can capture relative velocity differences in parallel motion.

evaluation.overall_instruction: Answer 'Yes' or 'No' to each question and briefly explain your reasoning for each.
evaluation.evaluation_description: Three reasoning layers are assessed: (1) Scene & viewpoint consistency (q1–q2). (2) Relative motion coherence (q3–q5). (3) Physical & temporal realism (q6–q8).

evaluation_questions.question: Is the viewpoint clearly identifiable as being inside or associated with a moving system (drone flight view)?
Does the camera perspective reflect motion consistent with a drone’s movement, such as slight upward or downward tilt during flight?
Are relative motion directions between observer and background correct (parallel motion dynamics)?
Is the motion speed and direction physically plausible (parallel motion dynamics)?
Is parallax or motion depth correctly represented (parallel motion dynamics)?
Does the drone maintain a consistent forward motion without unexpected changes in speed or direction during the scene?
Are motions temporally consistent and smooth without abrupt reversals (velocity realism)?
Is the drone's camera angle consistently aligned with its forward movement, avoiding sudden shifts or tilts that contradict the drone's flight direction?

prompts.prompt_en: A camera pans across a moving river from a stationary bridge.
prompts.description: Tests how the model represents moving objects from a static observation point.

evaluation.overall_instruction: Answer 'Yes' or 'No' to each question and briefly explain your reasoning for each.
evaluation.evaluation_description: Three reasoning layers are assessed: (1) Scene & viewpoint consistency (q1–q2). (2) Relative motion coherence (q3–q5). (3) Physical & temporal realism (q6–q8).

evaluation_questions.question: Is the viewpoint clearly identifiable as being inside or associated with a moving system (stationary bridge)?
Does the camera maintain a consistent perspective relative to the stationary bridge throughout the pan?
Are relative motion directions between observer and background correct (unidirectional river flow)?
Is the motion speed and direction physically plausible (unidirectional river flow)?
Is parallax or motion depth correctly represented (unidirectional river flow)?
Does the river appear to flow smoothly in the direction consistent with the camera's pan movement throughout the video?
Are motions temporally consistent and smooth without abrupt reversals (static camera realism)?
Do lighting and reflections stay coherent with the direction of motion (static camera realism)?

prompts.prompt_en: A person spins around holding a camera while a fixed object appears to rotate around them.
prompts.description: Tests whether the model can generate apparent relative motion caused by camera rotation.

evaluation.overall_instruction: Answer 'Yes' or 'No' to each question and briefly explain your reasoning for each.
evaluation.evaluation_description: Three reasoning layers are assessed: (1) Scene & viewpoint consistency (q1–q2). (2) Relative motion coherence (q3–q5). (3) Physical & temporal realism (q6–q8).

evaluation_questions.question: Is the viewpoint clearly identifiable as being inside or associated with a moving system (spinning observer)?
Does the camera's point of view change in a way that reflects the person spinning around, indicating a moving reference frame?
Are relative motion directions between observer and background correct (rotating background)?
Is the motion speed and direction physically plausible (rotating background)?
Is parallax or motion depth correctly represented (rotating background)?
Does the internal frame remain stable relative to itself (camera rotation realism)?
Are motions temporally consistent and smooth without abrupt reversals (camera rotation realism)?
Does the apparent rotation of the fixed object accurately reflect the camera’s relative movement without unnatural lag or jitter?

prompts.prompt_en: A passenger looks out of a moving airplane window at stationary clouds below.

evaluation.overall_instruction: Answer 'Yes' or 'No' to each question and briefly explain your reasoning for each.
evaluation.evaluation_description: Three reasoning layers are assessed: (1) Scene & viewpoint consistency (q1–q2). (2) Relative motion coherence (q3–q5). (3) Physical & temporal realism (q6–q8).

evaluation_questions.question: Is the viewpoint clearly identifiable as being inside or associated with a moving system (airplane interior)?
Does the camera perspective maintain a consistent viewpoint from inside the airplane during the scene?
Are relative motion directions between observer and background correct (high-speed motion)?
Is the motion speed and direction physically plausible (high-speed motion)?
Is parallax or motion depth correctly represented (high-speed motion)?
Does the internal frame remain stable relative to itself (aerial realism)?
Are motions temporally consistent and smooth without abrupt reversals (aerial realism)?
Does the perspective of the clouds below remain consistent with the motion of the airplane throughout the video?

prompts.prompt_en: A person runs on a treadmill while the background remains still.
prompts.description: Tests whether the model understands motion as a relative-frame concept.

evaluation.overall_instruction: Answer 'Yes' or 'No' to each question and briefly explain your reasoning for each.
evaluation.evaluation_description: Three reasoning layers are assessed: (1) Scene & viewpoint consistency (q1–q2). (2) Relative motion coherence (q3–q5). (3) Physical & temporal realism (q6–q8).

evaluation_questions.question: Is the viewpoint clearly identifiable as being inside or associated with a moving system (treadmill setup)?
Does the video show the treadmill belt moving under the person's feet as they run?
Are relative motion directions between observer and background correct (self-motion vs static background)?
Is the motion speed and direction physically plausible (self-motion vs static background)?
Is parallax or motion depth correctly represented (self-motion vs static background)?
Does the internal frame remain stable relative to itself (stationary background realism)?
Does the treadmill's position and movement stay consistent relative to the person throughout the video?
Does the treadmill's surface appear flat and level throughout the video, maintaining a consistent orientation relative to the person?

prompts.prompt_en: A drone circles around a statue, making the background appear to move.

evaluation_questions.question: Is the viewpoint clearly identifiable as being inside or associated with a moving system (orbiting drone)?

Is parallax or motion depth correctly represented (background parallax shift)?
Does the internal frame remain stable relative to itself (circular path realism)?
Are motions temporally consistent and smooth without abrupt reversals (circular path realism)?
Does the drone maintain a consistent relative distance from the statue throughout the circling motion?

prompts.prompt_en: A car drives past streetlights that seem to move backward rapidly.

evaluation.overall_instruction: Answer 'Yes' or 'No' to each question and briefly explain your reasoning for each.

evaluation_questions.question: Is the viewpoint clearly identifiable as being inside or associated with a moving system (moving car interior)?
Does the video show the streetlights' movement relative to the car in a way that indicates the car is moving forward?
Are relative motion directions between observer and background correct (backward parallax motion)?
Is the motion speed and direction physically plausible (backward parallax motion)?
Is parallax or motion depth correctly represented (backward parallax motion)?
Do the streetlights appear to move consistently backward relative to the moving car throughout the scene?
Are motions temporally consistent and smooth without abrupt reversals (forward velocity realism)?
Do the streetlights' positions and sizes maintain consistent spatial relationships to each other as they move in the scene?

prompts.prompt_en: A person rotates slowly in a chair while the room appears to spin around them.
prompts.description: Tests whether the model can generate visual consistency in a rotating frame.

evaluation.overall_instruction: Answer 'Yes' or 'No' to each question and briefly explain your reasoning for each.
evaluation.evaluation_description: Three reasoning layers are assessed: (1) Scene & viewpoint consistency (q1–q2). (2) Relative motion coherence (q3–q5). (3) Physical & temporal realism (q6–q8).

evaluation_questions.question: Is the viewpoint clearly identifiable as being inside or associated with a moving system (rotating chair)?

Is the motion speed and direction physically plausible (room rotation around axis)?
Is parallax or motion depth correctly represented (room rotation around axis)?
Does the room's spinning movement maintain a consistent direction and speed throughout the video?
Are motions temporally consistent and smooth without abrupt reversals (slow rotation realism)?

prompts.prompt_en: A cyclist films while riding, showing stationary buildings moving backward.

evaluation_questions.question: Is the viewpoint identifiable as being attached to a moving observer (cyclist-mounted camera)?
Is the stationary environment clearly visible as the relative background?
Are background objects moving in the opposite direction of travel (backward parallax)?
Is the backward motion speed physically plausible given the cyclist’s velocity?
Does parallax depth vary correctly between near and far buildings?
Is the camera vibration or viewpoint motion smooth and consistent with cycling?
Are motion transitions continuous without abrupt jumps or reversals?
Does the video maintain a consistent vertical orientation of the camera throughout the ride?

prompts.prompt_en: A person stands still as a moving walkway carries them forward.

Does the walkway’s mechanical motion remain visually consistent?
Is temporal continuity maintained without jumps or resets?
